{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.5, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: I flag a load-bearing construct error (the paper measures trust, not calibration), list specific fixes for the methods/results/discussion, and provide a rewritten draft that either narrows the claims to what the data support or, preferably, indicates the additional analyses needed.

## Core problem: the outcome measure does not operationalize calibration

Calibration of reliance is, by standard definition, the alignment between a user's trust/reliance behavior and the model's actual correctness on a per-item basis. The draft instead averages a single Likert trust rating per condition and treats "higher mean trust" as "better calibration." Those are different constructs, and under most operationalizations they are not even monotonically related:

- If the model is, say, 70% accurate, optimal calibration would yield mean reliance somewhere near 0.7 — neither the highest nor the lowest mean trust is automatically "best."
- The paper never reports the model's accuracy on the 30 items, nor whether participants discriminated between correct and incorrect answers. Without that, no condition can be said to "improve calibration."
- An equally consistent reading of the results is that verbal tags induced the most *overtrust* — i.e., the worst calibration — because they hide uncertainty behind soft language.

This is the single most important revision: either (a) reframe the paper as a study of self-reported trust and preference (not calibration), or (b) reanalyze with a true calibration metric (e.g., AUROC of reliance against correctness, Brier score against item accuracy, or reliance-appropriateness rates split by correct/incorrect trials).

## Specific issues to fix

1. **Discussion overreach.** Three claims do not follow from the reported evidence:
   - "Verbal confidence expressions improve trust calibration" — not measured.
   - "Participants form more accurate mental models … when shown coarse-grained verbal confidence" — mental-model accuracy was not assessed; preference ≠ accuracy.
   - "For end users, less precision can yield more understanding" — generalized from a single comparison without a comprehension or behavioral-accuracy measure.

2. **Missing verbal-vs-numeric contrast.** The conclusion privileges verbal over numeric, but only contrasts against control are reported. Add the pairwise verbal–numeric contrast with effect size and CI; the present text implies it without showing it.

3. **Model-side calibration not characterized.** Whether the "72% confident" tags were themselves calibrated against the model's accuracy is a precondition for interpreting user response to them. State how the model produced these signals (prompted self-report? logit-based?), and report their calibration (e.g., reliability diagram, ECE) on the 30 items.

4. **Design specification is thin.** "Within-subjects" with "condition order counterbalanced" leaves open how items map to conditions: was the same item shown in all three conditions to each participant (which would reveal the manipulation) or partitioned across conditions (Latin square)? Specify, and report any item-condition confounds.

5. **Statistics underreported.** Provide effect sizes (η²_p, Cohen's d_z for the pairwise contrasts), 95% CIs, sphericity correction, and treatment of the ordinal Likert outcome (cumulative-link mixed model would be more defensible than ANOVA on means).

6. **Sample considerations.** N=48 with three within-subjects conditions is reasonable for detecting a main effect but underpowered for interaction or moderation analyses; acknowledge this rather than letting the F-test stand alone.

7. **Prior work absent.** Place the study against the verbal-vs-numeric probability literature (Wallsten & Budescu; Zimmer) and recent LLM-confidence work (Lin et al. on verbalized uncertainty; Tian et al. on numeric self-confidence; Steyvers et al. and Vodrahalli et al. on user response to displayed LLM confidence; Zhou et al. on hedging language). The current framing reads as if no one has compared verbal and numeric LLM-confidence formats with users.

8. **"Trust" vs "reliance."** The Likert prompt asks about reliance ("How much would you rely on this answer?"), but the construct is labeled "trust." These are distinct in the trust-in-automation literature (Lee & See) — pick one and be consistent, or measure both.

## Suggested rewrite (narrowed to what the data support)

> Recent work has shown that large language models can be prompted to surface verbal or numeric confidence alongside their answers (Lin et al., 2022; Tian et al., 2023). Whether such expressions, when displayed in a chat interface, change end users' stated reliance — and whether verbal and numeric formats differ in this respect — remains open. We report a within-subjects experiment comparing three display formats.
>
> **Participants and task.** Forty-eight Prolific workers (ages 22–61, mixed technical backgrounds) completed 30 short trivia and reasoning items. Each item was answered by [model, version, prompting strategy]; per-item model accuracy was [X%] and the displayed confidence values were [calibration metric, e.g., ECE = …]. Items were partitioned across three display conditions using a Latin-square assignment counterbalanced over participants: (a) answer only (control); (b) answer plus a verbal tag ("high / medium / low"); (c) answer plus a numeric percentage (e.g., "72% confident"). Condition order was also counterbalanced.
>
> **Measures.** After each trial, participants rated reliance on a 5-point Likert item ("How much would you rely on this answer?"). We additionally [recorded a binary accept/reject decision / scored answer correctness against ground truth], allowing us to compute a reliance-appropriateness measure: AUROC of reliance ratings against item-level correctness, per participant per condition.
>
> **Results.** Mean reliance ratings differed by condition (verbal M=4.12, SD=0.61; numeric M=3.88, SD=0.72; control M=3.41, SD=0.80; cumulative-link mixed model, condition χ²(2)=…, p<.001; verbal vs. numeric d_z=…, 95% CI […]; verbal vs. control d_z=…; numeric vs. control d_z=…). Reliance-appropriateness (AUROC) was [report], with [pattern]. Post-task ratings of "naturalness" and "ease of acting on" favored the verbal format.
>
> **Discussion.** Displaying confidence — in either format — raised stated reliance relative to a no-confidence baseline, and the verbal format was preferred and produced the largest increase. Whether this preference reflects better-calibrated reliance or merely a more agreeable surface form depends on the reliance-appropriateness analysis: [interpretation contingent on AUROC results]. We caution against equating higher mean trust with better calibration; under a moderately accurate model, the verbal format's larger trust increase may also be consistent with overreliance, and the post-task preference data alone do not adjudicate this. Future work should pair display-format manipulations with model-side calibration manipulations to disentangle user response to *format* from user response to *signal quality*.

## Uncertainty disclosure

I have not verified the specific citations suggested (Lin et al. 2022; Tian et al. 2023; Wallsten & Budescu; Lee & See; Steyvers et al.; Vodrahalli et al.; Zhou et al.) against current bibliographic records — they reflect literatures I expect to be relevant; please confirm exact references before insertion. The rewritten results paragraph contains bracketed placeholders for analyses (AUROC, model-side ECE, effect sizes, CIs) that the original draft did not report; whether those analyses are feasible depends on data you collected but did not describe here (notably per-trial correctness and per-trial reliance, rather than per-condition means).
